coredata.dc:title: Advances in polyphenol-based carbon dots for biomedical engineering applications

Hong, Jinwoo
Lee, Hyojun
Choi, Sumi
Shin, Kwangsoo
Gu, Minsu
Kim, Su-Hwan
coredata.dc:description: Polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs) are a promising class of nanomaterials that have been increasingly studied for their potential applications in biomedical engineering. PCDs possess inherent therapeutic effects due to the polyphenolic nature of their composition, including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Functionalization of PCDs with biologically active molecules can enhance these therapeutic effects, making them useful in drug delivery applications. Moreover, the electron transfer of phenolic groups allows PCDs to easily convert light to heat or fluorescence, making them versatile agents for photothermal therapy and multimodal imaging. PCDs can also be used in biosensors, as their surface functional groups can interact with analytes such as metal ions, antibodies, and enzymes, resulting in changes in fluorescence emission. Recent studies have demonstrated the potential of PCDs in a wide range of applications, including drug delivery, tissue regeneration, and bioimaging. Despite their promising potential, further research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms of action and optimize the synthesis and functionalization of PCDs. Nonetheless, the increasing interest in PCDs as a new class of nanomaterials for biomedical applications indicates that they would play an important role in the development of new therapeutic and imaging strategies in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of structure and features of polyphenols (up left), PCDs (up right), and PCD applications in biomedical engineering (down). PCDs have distinguished features including surface functional groups, high water solubility, and electron transfer along with holding polyphenol features such as anti-oxidant, ROS scavenging, and anti-inflammation.

Schematic illustration of a plausible mechanism of biological functions of PCDs. PCDs can be delivered into cellular cytoplasm by endocytosis/clathrin-mediated endocytosis. Intracellular PCDs can scavenge intracellular ROS via phenolic hydroxy groups and inhibit pro-inflammatory factors. PCDs can also interact with TLRs following inhibition of TLR signal pathway.

Applications of PCDs as ROS scavengers, anti-inflammation, and wound healing. (A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PCDs-based on herbal tea polyphenol and their regulating immune reactions. (B) Western blot assay of iNOS and COX-2 to compare immunomodulation between herbal tea extraction and herbal tea-derived PCDs. (A, B) are adapted with permission [62]. Copyright 2022 American Chemical Society. (C) Schematic illustration of quenching surface functional groups (CO and –NH2) to evaluate whether surface functional groups affect ROS scavenging. (D) DPPH and ABTS assay. Surface quenched groups (BWE-CDs-EG and BWE-CDs-BOC) exhibited decreased ROS scavenging activity compared to BWE-CDs. (C, D) are adapted with permission [22]. Copyright 2023 American Chemical Society. (E) Schematic for the synthesis of PCDs-based on phloroglucinol for wound healing. (F) In vitro anti-aging properties evaluating inhibition of skin enzyme activity. (G) Representative H&E images of the mouse in vivo wound healing. PCD-incorporated gelatin hydrogel promoted neovascularization, and growth of epidermal ridges and hair follicles. (E, F, G) are adapted with permission [16]. Copyright 2022 American Chemical Society.

Applications of PCDs as deliver vehicles for anti-cancer drugs, anti-rheumatoid arthritis drugs, and therapeutic agents. (A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PCDs-based on phloroglucinol (g-CQDs) for pH-triggered Dox delivery. (B) Representative H&E images of tumor after treatment of g-CQDs@Dox to B16F10 tumor-bearing mice. The g-CQDs@Dox group showed tumor apoptosis, mainly in the nucleus. (A, B) are adapted from permission [63]. Copyright 2022 Royal Society of Chemistry. (C) Ultrasonographic images of rat joints. (a), (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) indicate normal rats, FCA-induced rats, ibuprofen-treated rats, thymol-treated rats, and CD-T-treated rats, respectively. CD-T-treated rats exhibited significant recovery from RA. (D) RA score of each group. (C, D) are adapted from permission [75]. Copyright 2021 Royal Society of Chemistry. (E) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of PCDs-based on chlorogenic acid (ChA CQDs) and GSH oxidase-mimetic reactions of ChA CQDs. (E) is adapted from permission [76]. Copyright 2022 American Chemical Society.

Applications of PCDs as bioimaging and biosensors. (A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PCDs-based on miswak polyphenols (Mis-mPD-CDs) for imaging live cells and detecting intracellular congo red in vitro and in vivo. The green fluorescence was detected in mis-mPD-CDs-treated cells, and it was quenched by congo red. (B) Representative images of zebrafish treated with congo red and Mis-mPD-CDs. (A, B) are adapted from permission [65]. Copyright 2022 Elsevier. (C) Schematic representation of the mechanism of mercury detection of vinyl functionalized PCDs-based on kaempferol polyphenols (V-CKCDs) and fluorescence enhancement by cysteine. (D) Quantification of PL intensity of V-CKCDs treated with various metal ions. V-CKCDs showed selectivity with mercury ions. (E) Representative images of zebrafish treated with V-CKCDs with mercury ions and cysteine. Mercury ions decreased the blue fluorescence, and cysteine enhanced the fluorescence. (C, D, E) are adapted from permission [15]. Copyright 2022 Elsevier.

Applications of PCDs as photothermal therapy (PTT), and photodynamic therapy (PDT) (A) Schematic representation of the preparation of PCDs-based on gallic acid doped with manganese (MP-CDs) and their mechanism on PTT and immunity activation. (B) Infrared thermal images of MP-CDs under 808 nm NIR laser. (C) Quantification of flow cytometry for expression of CD 80 and CD86 on the surface of DC2.4 cells. (A, B, C) are adapted from permission [17]. Copyright 2022 Biomedcentral, Part of Springer Nature. (D) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of PCDs-based on curcumin (ST-CQDs) and their photodynamic antibacterial effect. (E) S. aureus viability on different concentrations of ST-CQDs under 405 nm laser. (F) E. coli viability on different concentrations of ST-CQDs under 405 nm laser. (D, E, F) are adapted from permission [69]. Copyright 2023 Elsevier.
Comparison across the method of PCD synthesis, advantages, and disadvantages.

head.ce:title: Advances in polyphenol-based carbon dots for biomedical engineering applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs) are a promising class of nanomaterials that have been increasingly studied for their potential applications in biomedical engineering. PCDs possess inherent therapeutic effects due to the polyphenolic nature of their composition, including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Functionalization of PCDs with biologically active molecules can enhance these therapeutic effects, making them useful in drug delivery applications. Moreover, the electron transfer of phenolic groups allows PCDs to easily convert light to heat or fluorescence, making them versatile agents for photothermal therapy and multimodal imaging. PCDs can also be used in biosensors, as their surface functional groups can interact with analytes such as metal ions, antibodies, and enzymes, resulting in changes in fluorescence emission. Recent studies have demonstrated the potential of PCDs in a wide range of applications, including drug delivery, tissue regeneration, and bioimaging. Despite their promising potential, further research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms of action and optimize the synthesis and functionalization of PCDs. Nonetheless, the increasing interest in PCDs as a new class of nanomaterials for biomedical applications indicates that they would play an important role in the development of new therapeutic and imaging strategies in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been developed for decades for various biomedical engineering applications, such as drug delivery, imaging modalities, and photothermal therapy [1–4]. These quasi-spherical nanomaterials consist of carbon in the core, with sizes ranging from a few nanometers to several tens of nanometers, and are synthesized using a variety of carbon-based precursors, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, graphite, and organic compounds, including small molecules, aromatic molecules, and polymers, through simple and low-cost methods [5–7]. The optical and physicochemical physical properties of CDs, such as strong fluorescence, high optical and chemical stability, and functional versatility with chemical modification, make them attractive for biomedical applications, including tissue engineering, imaging contrast agent, and drug delivery vehicle [4,8]. Due to the high surface area-to-volume ratio of CDs, they are ideal for interaction with cells and tissues and for the delivery of bioactive agents, such as drugs or growth factors. Moreover, CDs can be functionalized with specific molecules to target specific cells or tissues, allowing for the targeted delivery of bioactive agents and improving the efficacy of tissue engineering and other biomedical applications [9–11]. However, concerns have been raised about their potential toxicity, poor solubility, and biocompatibility, particularly for CDs synthesized with metal ions [12,13]. Since CDs in biological tissues interacts actively with the cells and biomolecules, the metal ions cause tissue damage and lead to a range of adverse effects including organ damage, and neurological disorders [13,14]. In addition, the major applications of CDs in biomedical applications mostly rely on their optical property, additional functionalization is needed to be used to have therapeutic efficacy. Therefore, biocompatible materials-based CDs should be researched to enhance biological activity and modulate side effects.

Recently, a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial called polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs), has been developed and applied in various biomedical engineering [15–17]. PCDs are derived from polyphenols and are characterized by their small size, high surface area, and unique optical properties, such as fluorescence [18,19]. Since polyphenols are derived from naturally occurring compounds found in plants, the use of polyphenols as a carbon source promotes a green and environmentally friendly synthesis process, aligning with sustainable practices in materials science. In addition to the reactive oxygen scavenging property of polyphenols, PCDs have several advantages over polyphenol molecules, such as water solubility, enhanced biocompatibility, improved antioxidant activity, and anti-inflammatory effect [20–22]. Polyphenols are hydrophobic due to their molecular structure containing multiple hydrophobic functional groups, such as aromatic rings and alkyl chains, which make them resistant to being solvated in water and can limit their bioavailability and efficacy when they are administered [23]. However, PCDs are typically hydrophilic due to the presence of functional groups on their surface, such as carboxylic acids and amines, which provide a polar surface that facilitates the interaction with the polar solvent of water, leading to their solubilization in physiological media. The hydrophilic nature of PCDs can be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, which can form a hydrogen bond with water molecules [24,25]. Additionally, a high surface area, hydrophilic functional groups, and colloidal stability of PCDs make them more accessible to interact with biomolecules, resulting in improved antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Some polyphenols possess inherent bioactivity and health benefits, which could be retained or enhanced in the resulting carbon dots, opening up possibilities for targeted drug delivery and bioactive therapeutic applications. Hence, PCDs are promising materials for applications in tissue engineering and biomedical engineering over metal-based CDs.

Herein, we introduce PCDs and their applications in biomedical engineering based on these attractive features of PCDs as therapeutic agents along with imaging agents. This review covers a brief introduction to polyphenols for biomedical engineering, various synthesis methods of PCD, chemical and biological properties of PCDs suitable for biomedical engineering, and applications of PCDs in biomedical engineering, including cancer treatment, inflammatory disease, wound healing, connective tissue engineering, and biosensors. Finally, we discuss future perspectives of PCDs, providing a new class of materials with innovative features for biomedical engineering. We demonstrated that this review would promote research on polyphenols-based carbon dots, which offer advantages for tissue engineering and biomedical applications, surpassing the limitations of conventional carbon dot sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polyphenols for biomedical Engineering: Natural ROS Scavenger
ce:section.ce:para: Polyphenols are a group of naturally occurring compounds that are widely distributed in plant foods such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, nuts, and tea [26,27]. They are known for their potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Polyphenols have a wide range of health benefits, including reducing the risk of chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and neurodegenerative disorders [28–30]. They also have potential applications in the tissue engineering and biomedical engineering industries [31–33]. Several subclasses of polyphenols, including flavonoids, stilbenes, and phenolic acids, have unique chemical structures and biological activities. The general chemical structure of polyphenols consists of one or more hydroxyl (OH) groups attached to an aromatic ring structure [34,35]. The number and position of these hydroxyl groups determine the specific type of polyphenol and its biological activity. Additionally, polyphenols can be further divided into subclasses based on the number and type of aromatic rings in their structure [36,37]. For example, flavonoids have a characteristic three-ring structure, while stilbenes have a two-ring structure with a carbon–carbon double bond. Some polyphenols also have additional functional groups, such as ester or ether linkages, which can affect their biological activity [36,37]. Overall, the diversity of the chemical structures of polyphenols contributes to their wide range of biological activities.

Polyphenols have structural features in the functional group that can endow them with chemical and biological properties that make them applicable in biomedical engineering. In normal physiological conditions, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are produced in human tissues as a byproduct of normal cellular metabolism [38,39]. During cellular metabolism, the mitochondria produce ROS as part of the process of generating energy in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). In addition, ROS can be produced through various enzymatic reactions, such as the activation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidase, which is involved in the oxidative burst of phagocytes [40,41]. However, environmental factors such as hypoxia, nutrient deprivation, mechanical strain, ultraviolet radiation, exposure to toxic chemicals, and inflammation can also increase the levels of ROS in tissues [42–44]. While some ROS is necessary for normal cellular function, excessive production of ROS can lead to oxidative stress and cellular damage, which can contribute to the development of various diseases. The multiple hydroxyl groups in polyphenols can act as electron donors to react with ROS such as free radicals and thereby effectively neutralize them, preventing oxidative damage to cellular components such as lipids, proteins, and DNA [45,46]. Additionally, polyphenols can also inhibit the activation of pro-inflammatory enzymes such as cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase (LOX), as well as the production of pro-inflammatory mediators which are involved in inflammation such as prostaglandins and leukotrienes [47–49]. Moreover, polyphenols can modulate the expression of various cytokines and transcription factors related to the regulation of inflammation, such as nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) [32,50]. By modulating these various pathways, polyphenols can reduce the severity and duration of inflammation, making them useful in the management of various inflammatory conditions. In addition to their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, polyphenols also stimulate the growth of new blood vessels, which is essential for tissue repair and regeneration. They also enhance the deposition of extracellular matrix (ECM) components that provide support for cells and help maintain tissue structure [51,52].

Since the primary role of polyphenols is to scavenge ROS with hydroxyl groups, the application of polyphenols in biomedical engineering often focuses on treating diseases associated with excessive ROS production due to tissue damage or abnormal immune responses. For example, polyphenols, such as resveratrol, quercetin, and epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) have been used in wound dressings to promote wound healing by scavenging ROS, stimulating cell proliferation, and extracellular matrix (ECM) deposition [33,53]. Zhao et al. verified that resveratrol from blueberries improved wound healing by enhancing angiogenesis (the growth of new blood vessels) and reducing oxidative stress [20]. Additionally, the authors found that local application of resveratrol resulted in a reduction in the expression of the protein p16INK4a, which has been linked to aging and impaired wound healing. Velde et al. investigated that anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin from strawberries reduced the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-1β, both in vitro and in vivo [21]. This reduction in cytokine levels likely leads to a reduction in inflammation at the wound site, which is known to impair wound healing. Another example is inflammation-mediated cartilage degeneration. Riegsecker et al. studied the effects of EGCG in vitro and in vivo in mouse models of rheumatoid arthritis [23]. The authors found that EGCG was able to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-1β, which are involved in the inflammation associated with rheumatoid arthritis. EGCG was delivered orally to the mice in the study, and the authors observed that EGCG improved blood vessel function and reduced oxidative stress in the vessels, which are important factors in the development of vascular inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis. Other examples are tissue adhesives, osteogenesis, neovascularization, and neural tissue engineering [32,54–56].

Along with the beneficial biological properties of polyphenol itself, polyphenol-based carbon dot (PCD) has several advantages over conventional polyphenols (Fig. 1 ). First, PCDs have improved solubility in aqueous solutions due to their hydrophilic surface functional groups [23]. Second, PCDs show higher stability in various environmental conditions such as temperature, pH, and light compared to polyphenols, which are susceptible to degradation and oxidation [57]. Third, PCDs have unique optical properties that polyphenol does not have. PCDs can convert various ranges of near-infrared light (NIR) to fluorescent light and heat, allowing them applicable in bioimaging and photothermal therapy (PTT) [15,17,25]. It is demonstrated that PCDs would be promising materials for biomedical engineering with biological functions as well as exceptional physicochemical properties.

Carbon dots (CDs) can be synthesized using various methods, broadly classified as “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [58,59]. The “top-down” method involves the cleaving or breaking down of sheet-like or bulky carbon materials, such as carbohydrates, carbon soot, carbon fibers, activated carbon, etc., through chemical, electrochemical, or physical approaches. This process relies on the breakdown of carbon–carbon bonds in these larger carbonaceous materials. Strong oxidizing agents like H2SO4 or HNO3 can oxidize these materials, leading to the formation of nanoscale CDs. Laser Ablation and Chemical Etching/Exfoliation are two commonly used top-down synthesis methods for carbon dots, which involve fragmentation or size reduction of larger carbonaceous materials to obtain smaller carbon dots [60].

In contrast, the “bottom-up” strategy is widely employed in the synthesis of polyphenol-derived carbon dots (PCDs). This method involves the pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic molecules, typically following four stages: condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation. Most polyphenolic compounds consist of small organic molecules, making them suitable for bottom-up conversion to PCDs at high temperatures. Reactions involving specific functional groups (–NH2 and –OH) play a crucial role in PCD formation. Commonly used bottom-up synthesis methods for carbon dots include hydrothermal, microwave, solvothermal, thermal decomposition, and sonochemical methods.

Considering the size range of polyphenols, they possess sub-angstrom dimensions that are well-suited for bottom-up approaches. While top-down approaches may be applicable in the synthesis of PCDs, an additional bottom-up process is necessary after breaking down the carbon–carbon bonds in polyphenols. Therefore, bottom-up approaches offer advantages in terms of cost, synthesis time, and stability of PCDs.

To date, polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs) have been synthesized through a variety of methods, including the hydrothermal method, microwave method, and solvothermal method [16,25,61]. Unfortunately, sonochemical and thermal decomposition methods have not yet been reported in PCD synthesis. However, these synthesis techniques, which involve the formation of carbon–carbon bonds, are considered feasible, and we anticipate that the synthesis of polyphenol-based carbon dots will be extensively explored in future research. As each method has different reaction conditions, kinetics, and equipment, the choice of method depends on the desired properties, reaction rate, expected by-products, and potential applications. We summarized the advantages and disadvantages of each method (Table 1 ).

The hydrothermal method involves mixing carbon precursors with a hetero-atom doping agent such as N, P, and S in a water-based solution and heating the mixture under high pressure and temperature conditions [16,18,62–65]. Typically, the hydrothermal method is carried out at high temperatures, ranging from 150 °C to 250 °C, and high pressures, ranging from 1 to 20 atm. The exact conditions used can affect the size, shape, and optical properties of the produced PCDs. This method is simple and inexpensive compared to other methods, as well as eco-friendly, which utilizes water as the reaction medium [22,24]. It can produce high yields of PCDs, making them a practical and scalable synthesis method [24]. A wide range of polyphenols, including naturally occurring and synthetic compounds such as catechin, anthocyanin, and quercetin can be used to synthesize PCD with this method [16,62–64]. The hydrothermal method is a straightforward synthesis method that does not require specialized equipment or techniques. However, the hydrothermal method involves exposure to corrosive environments such as high temperature and oxidation, which can cause thermal and oxidative degradation of polyphenols following the formation of by-products.

The microwave method involves mixing carbon precursors with a doping agent and exposing the mixture to microwave radiation to generate PCDs [4,66]. The microwave method allows for rapid heating and reaction times, reducing the synthesis time compared to the other methods [1,24]. The practical and scalable microwave method is a simple and straightforward synthesis method that does not require specialized equipment or techniques [67]. The hydrothermal and microwave methods are two commonly used techniques for the synthesis of PCDs but have differences in reaction rate, temperature, and reactivity of reaction carbon precursors. The reaction rate in the microwave method is much faster than the hydrothermal method, and the hydrothermal method requires much higher pressure. The reactivity of carbon precursors is higher in microwave synthesis, which affects reaction kinetics, so the synthesis of PCDs is more controllable in the hydrothermal method than in the microwave method. Adjusting reaction kinetics is quite important in PCD synthesis since it can affect the size, chemical, and optical properties of PCDs. Additionally, high reactivity in the short-time microwave method leads to the formation of by-products, including impurities, aggregates, and oxidation products [67].

The solvothermal method is similar to the hydrothermal method in terms of being conducted at high temperature and high pressure, but different in the reaction medium. It employs organic solvents as a reaction medium, which can enhance the solubility and reactivity of the hydrophobic polyphenols [3]. The solvents used in the solvothermal method include ethanol, methanol, and acetone, ionic liquids such as 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate, and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and dimethylformamide (DMF) [67]. Since the solubility and reactivity in the solvothermal reaction are improved due to the use of organic solvent as reaction medium, the formation of stable covalent carbon–carbon bonding between precursors with phenolic hydroxyl groups is facilitated in solvothermal reaction, and the produced PCDs are highly stable [68,69]. However, since the typical solvents are highly cytotoxic, an extensive solvent removal process is needed to be applicable in biomedical engineering. Additionally, the solvothermal method is also exposed to corrosive environments similar to the thermal method resulting in thermal and oxidative degradation of polyphenols [2].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical features of polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and PCDs are both nanoscale carbon-based materials that share properties such as size, structure, and fluorescent properties, but they differ in their chemical features. CDs are generally composed of a mixture of carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen atoms, whereas PCDs are made of carbon and oxygen atoms with covalently bonded polyphenolic moieties [16,70,71]. Since reactive nitrogen is presented on CDs and PCDs have polyphenolic groups, PCDs tend to have higher chemical stability compared to CDs for biological applications [67,72]. The physicochemical property of PCDs can vary including size, structure, surface area, surface functional groups, and optical properties depending on the type of polyphenols and synthesis methods used.

The physicochemical properties of PCD can vary depending on the type of polyphenol and the synthesis method, including size, structure, surface area, functional groups, and optical characteristics. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) can be utilized to observe the size and morphology of carbon dots, and lattice fringes can help determine the crystal planes corresponding to carbon dots, with complementary analysis often performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Typically, the composition of PCD can be further analyzed using techniques such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). FT-IR can provide valuable information about functional groups and is an essential method for characterizing material properties, helping to determine some or all the molecular types and structures. XPS is a surface analysis technique primarily used to characterize the surface elements and chemical states of materials. According to numerous reports on FT-IR and XPS analysis of PCD, many parts of carbon dots contain functional groups such as 3398 cm−1 (phenolic OH), 1640 cm−1 (aromatic CC), CH, CO, and CC. Additionally, PCD generally contains varying amounts of nitrogen and sulfur elements, in addition to carbon and oxygen elements [73]. The optical properties of PCD can be analyzed using techniques such as UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis Spectroscopy) and photoluminescence (PL). Most PCDs exhibit absorption bands around 260–323 nm, regardless of the synthesis method, and under excitation at wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm, they show different emission colors. However, there might be slight absorption shoulders in the absorption spectra related to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds or n-π* transitions of CO bonds in PCDs. The surface passivation of PCDs has been shown to influence the absorption of PCDs, leading to a shift in the absorbance to longer wavelengths [74].

PCDs are usually nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. The small size of PCDs in biomedical engineering can provide several advantages. The small size of PCDs allows for better permeation and penetration into tissue, making them useful for various applications, especially in drug delivery [16,63,68,75]. The large surface area can increase the number of functional groups available for chemical reactions and the loading capacity of therapeutic agents, leading to further chemical modification and extensive functionalization, and greater versatility in biomedical engineering applications [9,76]. The small size of carbon dots can reduce their toxicity compared to larger nanoparticles, making them safer for use in biomedical applications.

The general structure of PCDs is composed of a core of carbon, surrounded by an outer shell composed of oxygen. The carbon core of PCDs is typically composed of graphene-like sheets, which gives PCDs their unique optical and electronic properties [5,75]. The outer shell of PCDs is composed of oxygen, which is bonded to the carbon core through covalent bonds. This outer shell is often responsible for the solubility, biocompatibility, fluorescence, and antioxidant properties of PCDs.

Various functional groups on the surface of PCDs, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl phenols, or amino groups, can be used to modify their properties or functionalize them for specific applications. Surface functional groups on PCDs can provide several advantages in biomedical engineering [63]. First, the presence of functional groups can improve the biocompatibility of PCDs by reducing toxicity and improving interactions with biological systems [19]. The second is to improve the stability of PCDs in biological systems, reducing aggregation and degradation over time. Third, it can be used to induce PCDs in specific tissues or cells, improving the delivery and efficacy of therapeutic agents in biomedical engineering [76].

PCDs can exhibit strong fluorescence depending on the size of carbon core and surface functional groups and can be used as fluorescent probes for imaging and sensing applications. It is because PCDs have plenty of polyphenol’s derivatives, which absorb light and emit fluorescence, and a high surface-to-volume ratio, which leads to a large number of fluorescent sites in a single PCD. The optical properties of PCDs in biomedical engineering provide several advantages. The fluorescence of PCDs can be used for imaging and visualization of tissues, cells, and therapeutic agents in biomedical engineering applications [57,77]. The high fluorescence intensity of PCDs can lead to improved sensitivity in imaging and detection, allowing for improved visualization of small or low-concentration targets. PCDs have low toxicity compared to traditional fluorescent dyes, making them safer for use in biomedical engineering applications. PCDs can be functionalized with additional fluorescent labels, allowing for multiplexed imaging and tracking of multiple targets in biomedical engineering. These distinguished properties of PCDs compared to polyphenols include their ability to serve as imaging agents for multiple imaging modalities, including fluorescence imaging, which improves the accuracy and versatility of tissue imaging and analysis [18,19,57,77–80].

Additionally, PCDs can be used in photothermal therapy [17,61,69,81]. When exposed to light, PCDs can absorb light energy due to the presence of aromatic structures in their carbon cores, resulting in the conversion of light energy to thermal energy. There are several proposed mechanisms of energy conversion. First, the excited electrons in PCDs relax to their ground state, releasing the excess energy as heat, which is called non-radiative relaxation. Second, intersystem crossing whose excited electrons can cross from the singlet state to the triplet state can lead to energy transfer to other molecules, producing heat [68]. Third, the collective oscillation of electrons in PCDs produces localized heating. The light absorption properties of PCDs depend on surface chemistry and the wavelength of the light. This increase in local temperature can induce protein denaturation, membrane damage, and DNA damage in cancer cells, leading to apoptosis [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological features of polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The biological features of PCDs make them promising materials for biomedical engineering. PCDs share common biological features with polyphenols, such as antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. However, the physicochemical features of PCDs, including surface area, functional groups, oxidative stability, and structural stability, provide additional functional biological activities compared to polyphenol molecules. As PCDs are synthesized from natural, biodegradable polyphenols, they have been shown to exhibit low toxicity in various cells. The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of PCDs improve their interaction with biological systems, resulting in improved biocompatibility.

The synthesis of polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs) involves the decomposition or degradation of polyphenols, occurring under high temperature and pressure conditions. However, the hydroxyl phenol groups, which are responsible for the biological activity, remain unaffected by decomposition or degradation. Instead, it is the carbon–carbon bonds that link the hydroxyl phenol groups together that undergo cleavage. For instance, non-acylated anthocyanins, found in fruits and vegetables, can yield phloroglucinaldehyde via C3-C4 cleavage and phenolic acids via C2-C3 cleavage during thermal oxidative degradation [82]. This decomposition process transforms the bulky polyphenol structure of anthocyanins into a phenolic hydroxy group with an –OH group. Remarkably, the phenolic hydroxy groups can maintain their structure even at high temperatures, demonstrating their potential as thermal stabilizers. In studies by Yang et al., incorporating phenol additives improved the high-temperature stability of polymers by forming hydrogen bonds with biodegradable polymers, delaying their thermal decomposition, and benefiting from the radical scavenging activity of polyphenols [83]. Additionally, this decomposition is typically accompanied by the addition of a crosslinker, which facilitates the reconnection of polyphenol fragments. FT-IR data reveals that, during this decomposition, phenol-phenol linkages break down, while the aromatic ring inherent to phenol remains intact [57]. This unique characteristic makes PCD synthesis suitable for preserving stable biological activity. It should be noted that when using organic solvents in other types of synthesis, there is a risk of breaking down these aromatic rings. However, in the case of PCD synthesis, which typically employs water-based solvents, the ring structure remains intact. In situations where organic solvents like formamide are used, there may be slight ring degradation, but the solvent itself does not significantly cleave CC bonds. Consequently, the biological activity of polyphenols remains well-preserved even after the synthesis of PCDs.

PCDs can be easily taken up by cells due to their small size and surface functionalization, making them useful for cellular imaging and tracking [78,79] (Fig. 2 ). They can be internalized into cells through a process called endocytosis [84]. Endocytosis is the uptake of substances by cells, and there are several types of endocytosis, including phagocytosis, pinocytosis, and clathrin-mediated endocytosis. In the case of PCDs, they can enter cells through clathrin-mediated endocytosis. This process involves the formation of clathrin-coated pits on the cell membrane, which then invaginate and form vesicles that contain the PCDs. The vesicles then move into the cytoplasm and eventually reach their target within the cell. The targeting of cellular organelles differs from the types of polyphenols [78–80].

Similar to polyphenols, PCDs have antioxidant properties by scavenging free radicals, which are reactive species that can cause oxidative stress and damage to cells [22,85] (Fig. 2). The polyphenols present in PCDs can act as free radical scavengers, reducing the levels of oxidative stress and improving cell viability. Additionally, the carbon core of the PCDs also contributes to their antioxidant properties, as it has been shown to reduce oxidative stress in cells [16,57]. The enhanced antioxidant properties of PCDs compared to polyphenols are still under investigation. Some studies have suggested that PCDs may have improved antioxidant properties due to their unique chemical and physical properties, such as their high surface area. However, further research is needed to fully understand the relationship between the antioxidant properties of PCDs and their physicochemical properties.

The anti-inflammatory properties of PCDs are believed to be due to the presence of phenolic groups and antioxidant activity in polyphenols [16,62]. PCDs have been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, which are involved in the inflammation response [75,86] (Fig. 2). Additionally, PCDs have been shown to inhibit the activation of NF-κB, a key transcription factor that regulates the expression of inflammatory genes [70]. In addition to indirect effects, PCDs can interact directly with cell surface receptors such as toll-like receptors (TLRs) [87,88]. The TLRs are important for recognizing foreign pathogens. When PCDs bind to TLRs on immune cells, they have been shown to modulate the expression of TLRs and inhibit the activation of TLR signaling pathways, resulting in reduced inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of polyphenol-based carbon dot (PCD) in biomedical engineering
ce:section.ce:para: PCDs have potential applications in treating disease through radical scavenging, anti-inflammation, photothermal agents, and delivery vehicles for drugs, as well as bio-imaging and bio-sensors. In this section, we introduced the recent research in PCDs for biomedical engineering including ROS scavengers, anti-inflammatory agents, drug delivery, therapeutic agents, photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, bioimaging, and biosensor (Table 2 ).

As we mentioned above, PCDs possess significant intrinsic anti-inflammatory properties by scavenging ROS, down-regulating pro-inflammatory cytokines, and modulating cellular responses via binding to cell receptors. Certain diseases, such as rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), and allergies, can cause immune hyperactivity due to chronic inflammation or autoimmune reactions. PCDs hold potential as therapeutic agents that can restore the balance of the immune system.

Fu et al. synthesized herbal tea-derived polyphenol-based PCDs (H-CD) to regulate immune reactions [62] (Fig. 3 A). Surface characterization revealed that the unique structure of polyphenols, such as the phenolic hydroxyl group, was well maintained on the surface of H-CD (Fig. 3A). RAW264.7 cells treated with H-CD showed up-regulated cellular proliferation and increased secretion of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and COX-2 enzymes, whose relevant products (prostaglandins (PGs) and NO) are responsible for immunomodulation (Fig. 3B). The mechanism of immunomodulation activity has not been established, but it might be due to the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the H-CD surface. Deng et al. explored the relationship between surface functional groups and radical scavenging using hydrothermally synthesized PCDs derived from broccoli [22] (Fig. 3C). Carbonyl (CO) and amine (–NH2) groups were chemically quenched, and the change in ROS scavenging was evaluated. From the data, carbonyl- and amine-quenched PCDs decreased ABTS+ scavenging by 21.9% and 48%, respectively (Fig. 3D). A few basic studies have supplemented the relationship between the phenolic hydroxyl group and immunomodulation. Ashikawa et al. demonstrated that piceatannol, an analog of resveratrol found in red grapes, has a phenolic hydroxyl group and anti-ifnflammatory effects, but rhaponticin, another analog of resveratrol, had no biological effect since it lacks a phenolic hydroxyl group [89]. PCDs can also directly penetrate cancer cells and disrupt cellular functions to induce apoptosis. Ben-Zichri et al. synthesized resveratrol-based PCDs through the hydrothermal method and applied them to disrupt mitochondrial function in cancer cells [70]. The study showed that resveratrol-based PCDs impaired mitochondrial functions in SH-SY5Y cells such as inhibition of cytochrome-C oxidase activity, mitochondrial membrane disruption, and an increase in intracellular calcium. The anti-cancer activity of resveratrol-based PCD was more extensive compared to curcumin and triphenyl phosphonium-based PCD.

Wang et al. synthesized phenol-like group functionalized graphene quantum dots (h-GQD) to treat acute kidney injury (AKI), by functionalizing the surface of graphene quantum dots with phenol-like groups [90]. The phenol-like functionality of h-GQD demonstrated potent antioxidant activity, leading to significantly improved ROS scavenging activity. In in vitro studies using human embryonic kidney cell line HEK-293 T cells, h-GQD effectively relieved oxidative stress induced by LPS in a concentration-dependent manner. The study further demonstrated that h-GQD acted as an efficient nanomaterial to restore kidney function with excellent antioxidant activity and remarkable biocompatibility in AKI mice. The incorporation of phenol-like groups proved to enhance ROS scavenging activity and enhance the therapeutic potential of carbon dots.

Thanks to their anti-inflammatory effects, PCDs have been applied in wound healing applications. Moniruzzaman et al. developed PCDs derived from phloroglucinol (CQDs) that were incorporated into a gelatin hydrogel for wound healing [16] (Fig. 3E). Inhibition of skin enzymes such as tyrosinase, elastase, and collagenase, whose activity is responsible for skin aging, and reducing oxidative stress, play important roles in skin wound healing (Fig. 3F). CQDs have large polyphenolic-polyaromatic domains and more abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups than previously synthesized PCDs. These large surface areas and phenolic tri-hydroxyl group effectively inhibited skin enzyme activities and scavenged intracellular ROS. When the CQDs-incorporated gelatin hydrogel was implanted in in vivo full-size skin defect model, it promoted the wound healing process by facilitating neovascularization, and growth of epidermal ridges and hair follicles (Fig. 3G). Bankoti et al. also synthesized PCDs derived from onions (OCND) through the hydrothermal method for skin wound healing [19]. Along with their antioxidant properties, OCND promoted fibroblast migration and in vivo wound healing process. Sharma et al. developed PCDs derived from curcumin (CurCD) that were incorporated into a protease-responsive hydrogel for wound healing [71]. CurCD has superior properties such as water solubility, stability, cell proliferation, neovascularization, and antibacterial activity compared to curcumin. In the mouse skin incision model, CurCD/hydrogel composite supported faster wound closure with angiogenesis and restoration of epithelium.

Across the PCDs-derived from any source, they showed significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in cell lines and in vivo applications. However, some research has lacked detail information about which specific polyphenols can be ROS scavengers. When PCDs are synthesized using whole extracts of fruits or vegetables, they might be composed of multiple carbon sources such as diverse polyphenols, carbohydrates, and lipids. This makes it difficult to develop homogenous quality and reproducible quantity of PCDs since molecular compositions of whole fruits or vegetables are season- and region-specific properties. To develop PCDs as medical grades for clinical trials, it is important to synthesize PCDs using known polyphenols for homogenous structures and propose a plausible mechanism of ROS scavenging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Delivery vehicles for drugs or therapeutic agents
ce:section.ce:para: Delivering drugs via PCDs is another potential application. Phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of PCDs can interact with diverse moieties in proteins, chemicals, and carbohydrates, allowing for numerous drugs to be loaded onto PCDs. Additionally, the interaction between PCDs and drug candidates is based on non-covalent bonds such as hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions, which can be easily dissociated in environmental changes, allowing for control of release kinetics. Furthermore, PCDs themselves can act as therapeutic agents that mimic natural enzymes such as glutathione (GSH) oxidase, catalase, or superoxide dismutase. When therapeutic PCDs penetrate tumor tissue, they induce ROS-based apoptosis in biological systems.

D’souza et al. synthesized PCDs using Daucus carota subsp. sativus (domesticated carrot) roots through the hydrothermal method to deliver anti-cancer drugs [64]. Carrot (Daucus carota L.) contains multiple carbon sources such as carbohydrates, carotenoids, and polyphenols. Mitomycin, an anti-cancer drug, was effectively bonded on the surface of carrot-derived PCDs through a hydrogen bond, and released under a mildly acidic environment similar to the tumor extracellular microenvironment (pH ∼ 6.8). Moreover, carrot-derived PCDs directly interacted with the MCR-7 cell membrane and internalized into cells. Dutta et al. also developed phloroglucinol, found in Ecklonia cava and Alpinia blepharocalyx, based PCDs (g-CQDs) through the thermal heating method to deliver doxorubicin (Dox) into tumor tissue [63] (Fig. 4 A, B). Dox was loaded on the surface of PCDs via electrostatic interactions (g-CQDs@Dox), resulting in a loading efficiency of up to 54.62% (Fig. 4A). g-CQDs@Dox readily penetrated the nucleus of tumor cells including B16F10 and MDA Cells. Since the tumor environment has acidic conditions, Dox was released during intracellular pH fluctuation and induced DNA damage, following apoptosis. Interestingly, g-CQDs themselves can generate intracellular ROS in tumor cells to induce mitochondrial stress. This system demonstrated that the anti-cancer activity of g-CQDs@Dox came from the dual mechanism of DNA damage by Dox and intracellular ROS by g-CQDs (Fig. 4B). This system also has beneficial effects of anti-tumor efficacy in in vivo animal models developed by subcutaneously injected melanoma tumor cells.

Besides cancer therapy, Murugesan et al. synthesized rose flower-derived PCDs via the hydrothermal method and functionalized the PCD surface with the polyphenolic phytocompound thymol (CDs-Thymol) to treat rheumatoid arthritis [75] (Fig. 4C). Rheumatoid arthritis is an auto-immune disease that causes long-term pain, swelling, and cartilage destruction. An FCA-induced rat arthritis model was developed, and CDs-Thymol was orally administered. The CDs-Thymol-treated groups showed significantly relieved clinical conditions, including cartilage destruction, tissue swelling, and bone damage. X-ray imaging also revealed that CDs-Thymol recovered arthritis from synovial inflammation, joint space arrangements, and cartilage deformation (Fig. 4D).

Kazemian et al. developed a novel pH-responsive lignin-based MOF (Metal-Organic Framework) composite for simulated oral delivery systems [91]. They synthesized the lignin-Cu-MOF (L/HKUST-1) hybrid material using a one-pot technique with water as the solvent at room temperature. The drug loading capacity and drug release behavior of HKUST-1 and L/HKUST-1 were investigated using ibuprofen (IBU) as a model oral drug. The L/HKUST-1 composite demonstrated pH-controlled drug release behavior, enhancing drug stability at low pH, such as in gastric fluid, and controlling drug release in the pH range of 6.8 to 7.4, which is similar to the intestinal pH. This pH-responsive drug release behavior was validated by studying the drug release of IBU at various pH levels (pH = 1.2, pH = 6.8, and pH = 7.4) in simulated body fluid (SBF) using HKUST-1 and L/HKUST-1 as drug delivery models.

PCDs can also act as therapeutic agents themselves. Zhao et al. developed coffee-derived PCDs through the hydrothermal method to induce cancer cell ferroptosis [76] (Fig. 4E). Across the main components of coffee, including chlorogenic acid (ChA), caffeic acid, and quinic acid, ChA-based PCDs (ChA CQD) were used in this study since ChA has been established in potent antioxidant and chemoprotective activities. Interestingly, ChA CQD has GSH oxidase-like activity that can lead to cellular apoptosis through oxidative stress (Fig. 4E). In in vitro 3D tumor model, ChA CQDs dramatically down-regulated the expression level of GSH through GSG oxidase-like activity resulting in the production of oxidized glutathione (GSSG) and oxidative stress to tumor tissue. Furthermore, although apoptosis markers were not significantly changed, ferroptosis markers (GPX4, NRF2, SLC7A11, and COX-2) were detected, indicating that ChA CQDs selectively evoked cellular death through ferroptosis.

To bridge from the laboratory to clinical settings, the stability and safety of PCDs in vivo should be addressed. PCDs might be susceptible to degradation or clearance by the immune system, which can limit their ability to reach and accumulate in the target tissue. It is expected that the phenolic hydroxyl group can interact with specific biological tissues to prolong tissue accumulation, but biological proteins can form a corona around PCDs, impacting the uptake and clearance of PCDs by cells and tissues. It has been demonstrated that engineering PCDs to minimize non-specific protein interactions and promote specific interactions with target tissues or proteins can improve their efficacy and reduce potential toxicity.

Bioimaging and biosensors are among the most prominent applications of polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs). In the field of bioimaging, CDs have been extensively employed, thanks to their unique optical properties. CD typically exhibits a higher Quantum Yield (QY) attributed to the close arrangement of C–C bonds, facilitating efficient electron transfer [92]. Their high surface-to-volume ratio and abundant fluorophores in single CDs contribute to excellent absorbance and high fluorescence emission.

Conversely, PCDs tend to have lower QY values due to the heterogeneous nature of their carbon sources. Nevertheless, despite this, PCDs still maintain a sufficiently high level of QY for various application fields, including bioimaging. Furthermore, PCDs offer several advantages over conventional CDs, not only in imaging but also in therapeutics and other applications, owing to their intrinsic anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Moreover, PCDs are considered to be more biocompatible and offer greater chemical flexibility in elaborating surface functional groups compared to CDs. These surface functional groups play a crucial role in designing PCDs as biosensors. When PCDs interact with analytes such as metal ions, antibodies, DNA, or enzymes, the PCD/analyte complex induces changes in the fluorescence emission of PCDs, enabling sensitive detection.

Hence, considering their multifunctional properties, PCDs hold significant promise over CDs in tissue engineering and various biomedical applications, providing a valuable alternative for advanced and versatile biomedical research.

Durrani et al. synthesized miswak (Salvadora perica tree)-derived PCDs (Mis-mPD-CDs) via the hydrothermal method to detect congo red (CR) in living cells [65] (Fig. 5 A). CR is an organic dye used in the textile, printing, and rubber industries, but it is regarded as a pollutant of the environment and a carcinogenic molecule [93]. Mis-mPD-CDs can detect CR based on their fluorescence quenching via the inner filter effect, and the distinguished property was high sensitivity and selectivity that biological molecules, metal ions, and other dyes cannot interact with particles (Fig. 5A). Mis-mPD-CDs can sense and image CR in zebrafish (Fig. 5B). Edison et al. developed ferric ion (Fe3+) detection PCDs-based on Prunus avium (N-CDs) synthesized via the hydrothermal method [18]. N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence and accumulated into MDA-MB-231 cells for bio-imaging. When Fe3+ was bound to the surface of N-CDs, blue fluorescence was quenched to detect Fe3+ ions in water. N-CDs had high selectivity to detect Fe3+ ions compared to other metal ions including Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr2+, Al2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+. Sangubotla et al. synthesized boronic acid-functionalized bottle gourd-derived PCDs (FBCDs) to detect dopamine and image human neuroblastoma cells [80]. FBCDs exhibited emission of blue fluorescence in SH-SY5Y cells. Since dopamine makes a covalent bond with boronic acid, PL was easily quenched even in live cells. From several studies, it was demonstrated that eco-friendly materials-based PCDs have the potential for use in biological imaging applications and may provide a more environmentally friendly alternative to traditional fluorescent materials.

In addition to in vitro bio-imaging and biosensors, PCDs have been used as fluorescence materials for in vivo imaging. Lakshmi et al. developed a biosensor for the dual detection of mercury and cysteine using kaempferol-derived PCDs (CKCDs) [15] (Fig. 5C). When CKCDs were functionalized with 4-vinal aniline, photoluminescence was switched from bright orange to strong-blue fluorescence. Vinyl-functionalized CKCDs (V-CKCDs) can detect mercury ions through electrostatic interactions between the phenolic hydroxyl group and mercury (Fig. 5D). Then, mercury ions interacted with the thiol group on cysteine, resulting in fluorescence enhancement. V-CKCD biosensors can be used for in vivo sensing in zebrafish larvae, providing a platform for investigating the toxicity of these substances in living organisms (Fig. 5E). Dias et al. synthesized PCDs using diverse fruits including kiwi, avocado, pear, and black pepper for in vivo bioimaging [77]. These fruit-based carbon dots have good biocompatibility and can be used for imaging live cells and tissues without causing any significant toxicity. The fluorescence properties of the carbon dots are also found to be stable and long-lasting, enabling clear and bright imaging. Interestingly, black pepper had lower cytotoxicity in normal cell lines such as human epithelial cells, adult kidney, and colorectal adenocarcinoma cells, but higher toxicity in vivo than the other PCDs. When fruit-derived PCDs were injected into zebrafish embryos, avocado-based PCDs retained in the eye and yolk sac at high rate indicating potential in in vivo bioimaging applications.

When considering the applications of carbon dots (CDs), practical applications such as bioelectronics stand out as particularly promising. Yu et al. have explored the use of CDs in bioelectronics, leveraging their unique electronic and physicochemical properties, including high electron-transfer efficiency, excellent stability, distinct optical absorption, and photoluminescence [94]. CDs rich in phenolic hydroxyl groups exhibit a negative zeta potential, introducing ample electrons to the CD's surface. In their study, CDs were introduced to enhance the bioelectricity produced by S. oneidensis MR-1 bacteria, and the absorbed CDs were observed throughout the cells, including the cytoplasm, periplasm, and inner and outer membranes. The incorporation of CDs into the cells led to an increase in biomass of microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) anodes, thereby improving the rate of direct extracellular electron transfer in bioelectrical systems. This was attributed to the increased cell electronegativity facilitated by CDs. Additionally, CDs were found to accelerate the metabolic rate of S. oneidensis MR-1 bacteria.

Conversely, the application of polyphenol-based carbon dots (PCDs) in bioelectronics has not been reported yet. However, considering that PCDs share similar functions and properties with CDs, it is reasonable to speculate that PCDs will be extensively studied in the field of bioelectronics. Their unique characteristics, including negative zeta potential and electron transfer efficiency, make PCDs promising candidates for various bioelectronic applications. Further research in this area is warranted to explore the full potential of PCDs in bioelectronics.

PCDs for bioimaging and biosensors have several points that should be addressed. Along with limitations in safety and stability, the PCDs have relatively weak fluorescence compared to other probes such as quantum dots or organic dyes. While PCDs are composed of eco-friendly, and biocompatible materials, their relatively low fluorescence intensity can limit their sensitivity for deep tissue imaging. Additionally, for biosensors, PCDs may exhibit cross-reactivity with other molecules in complex biological samples since phenolic hydroxyl groups have universal interactions with molecules. Further research is needed to optimize the sensitivity and specificity of PCDs-based biosensors.

The photosensitizer (PS) compound in PDT is a crucial element in the therapeutic effect. Upon receiving stimulation from visible light, the PS reacts photosensitively in the surrounding biological environment, generating ROS, thereby increasing cytotoxicity in target cells and inducing tissue damage, and even cell death. Many conventional PS compounds, such as porphyrins, phthalocyanines, and their derivatives, often face limitations due to factors like low resistance to photobleaching under light exposure, heavy metal effects, and low clearance rates from target tissues. PCD, as a new metal-free carbon nanomaterial, has been proposed as an alternative due to its abundant surface groups, excellent biocompatibility, exceptional photobleaching resistance, and tunable optical properties [95]. PCDs can efficiently convert light into heat due to their strong light absorption in the NIR region. Their biocompatibility and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface make them easy to functionalize with targeting agents. PCDs-based PTT and PDT also have been widely developed for cancer therapy and antibacterial activity as they can induce local hyperthermia and reduce apoptosis of normal cells [96].

Wu et al. synthesized manganese-coordinated gallic acid-based PCDs (MP-CDs) through the microwave method to induce immunogenic cell death [17] (Fig. 6 A). In addition to PTT by PCDs, manganese on PCDs can activate dendritic cells (DC) to promote maturation and enhance antigen presentation. Although photothermal conversion efficiency has a negative correlation between manganese concentration and photothermal performance, the temperature of the MP-CD solution was raised to 62.6 °C indicating effective temperature induction of apoptosis in tumor cells (Fig. 6B). When DC2.4 cells were treated with MP-CDs and 808 nm laser, they exhibited the typical damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) due to immunogenic cell death (ICD). Additionally, CD80 and CD86, indicators in activation of antigen-presenting cells, were increased by MP-CDs indicating promotion of DC cell maturation (Fig. 6C). Yang et al. developed EGCG and citric acid-based PCDs (T-CDs) through the solvothermal method to generate intracellular ROS by irradiation of LED light for cancer therapy [68]. Singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals were produced by exposure to LED light through electron and energy transfer reactions, and these ROS caused 4 T1 cell death. T-CDs also showed PDT performance in vivo tumor models decreasing tumor size.

Yan et al. developed curcumin-based PCDs (ST-JHCQD) through a solvothermal method for PDT and antimicrobial activity [69] (Fig. 6D). ST-JHCQDs effectively produced ROS under blue light and induced light-mediated antibacterial activity against the gram-positive and negative bacteria (E. Coli and S. aureus) (Fig. 6E, F). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed that ROS damage by ST-JHCQDs resulted in disturbance of cell membrane stability and leakage of intracellular molecules. Similar to ST-JHCQD, Wen et al. synthesized curcumin and citric acid-based PCDs (CDs-Cur) through the microwave method for PTT/PDT dual-mode antibacterial activity [61]. CDs-Cur generated singlet oxygen and ROS under blue light, and therapeutic heat under 808 nm irradiation. With the combination of blue light and 808 nm laser, both gram-positive and negative bacteria significantly induced apoptosis.

Zhang et al. developed a polydopamine-based nano-vaccine to overcome tumor resistance in Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) caused by hypoxia [97]. The formulated MSNs-ABC@PDA-OVA nano-vaccine exhibits excellent photothermal properties and effectively eliminates primary tumors. Under laser irradiation, the MSNs-ABC@PDA-OVA nano-vaccine achieves rapid antigen release and endosome escape, enhances antigen-presenting cell activation and maturation, promotes tumor drainage to lymph nodes, and induces potent anti-tumor immune responses. Impressively, a single injection of MSNs-ABC@PDA-OVA successfully eradicates melanoma tumors with a 75% cure rate when combined with a single round of Photothermal Therapy (PTT). Additionally, this treatment generates robust immunological memory, effectively suppressing tumor recurrence and lung metastasis.

While PCDs have several advantages that make them a promising candidate for PTT, some limitations need to be considered to develop clinical trials. First is the low photothermal conversion rate compared to the other PTT agents such as gold nanoparticles. High doses of PCDs or long irradiation times are required to achieve the desired therapeutic effect. Additionally, although the surface functionalization of PCDs makes them selectively accumulate in cancer cells theoretically, there is a lack of practical examples of targeting agents suitable for PCDs, and functionalized PCDs that can accumulate into specific tissues. A minor concern is the stability of PCDs under photothermal conditions. Since the PCDs-based PTT and PDT are state-of-the-art approaches, several pieces of research must be accumulated to advance the targeting method and enhancement of photothermal conversion efficiency.

PCDs represent a promising new class of nanomaterials in the field of biomedical engineering. Recent advances in the synthesis and characterization of PCDs have provided insights into their unique physical, chemical, and biological properties, which make them attractive candidates for a variety of biomedical engineering applications. Hydrophobic polyphenols have been reconstructed by adopting the synthesis method of CDs to make them hydrophilic, and to create imaging probes that maintain the therapeutic effects of polyphenols themselves. Recent research has demonstrated that the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of PCDs are key functional moieties that can down-regulate immune reactions and oxidation through electron transfer. In addition to ROS scavenging and direct cellular modulation, the non-specific interactions between the phenolic hydroxyl group of PCDs and biological molecules make PCDs potentially useful in drug delivery applications. PCDs can also be used as therapeutic agents for PTT, bioimaging modalities, and biosensors, which are not related to the natural properties of polyphenols. Benefiting from the electron donor activity of polyphenols and the high surface areas of nanoparticles, PCDs can absorb light and emit fluorescence or heat, resulting in prominent optical properties.

Despite the progress that has been made in understanding the properties and applications of PCDs, some potential concerns need to be addressed before PCDs can be approved for medical use. First, more studies are needed to fully understand the underlying mechanism of potential cytotoxicity. Biocompatibilities of PCDs vary depending on their surface area, charge, and functional groups. Although PCDs are regarded as non-cytotoxic in low concentrations, it has not been well established which factors are dominant in biocompatibility. Second, a single polyphenol, not a whole extract of fruits or vegetables, should be utilized to synthesize the homogenous quality of PCDs to verify the therapeutic effects of each PCD. The whole extract of organisms has multiple carbon sources, making it difficult to define functionalities since the compositions of carbon sources in the whole extract depend on season and regions. To expand the applications of PCDs, it is crucial to establish proper PCD functionalization, optimize synthesis conditions, control lateral dimensions, and ensure suitable surface chemistry, all of which are essential for achieving a high quantum yield and precise control over PCD synthesis to ensure uniformity. Third, balancing between low-fouling and loading drugs is important to reduce the non-specific binding of PCDs on biological tissue and improve drug-targeting ability. Extensive interactions of PCDs on biological tissues and proteins suffer from protein corona formation to reduce therapeutic effects. Last, biodegradability and biosafety in vivo should be clarified. PCDs are generally biodegradable, but their clearance pathway or tissue-specific accumulation has been under studied. PCDs might be eliminated via renal clearance, hepatic clearance, or a combination of both. Further studies are needed since tissue accumulation, clearance pathway, and related safety are critical factors for clinical translation.

Overall, this paper provides an overview of the recent advances in PCDs and their applications in biomedical engineering. Along with the continuous development of various polyphenol-based carbon dots and the accumulation of pre-clinical studies in animal models, we believe that PCDs will provide practical solutions for clinical translations in biomedical engineering, bioimaging, and biosensors in the future.
